Even with its outsized gains, however, MGNR does not make it into the top quartile of natural resource ETFs on Morningstar. The gains from 2025, the only full year the fund has existed, were only good enough to get MGNR into the 31st percentile out of 128 other natural resource funds. These returns from the fund do not come without great risk as well. This American Beacon ETF is currently assigned a Morningstar risk score of 107, well above that of the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) and the Vanguard Growth Index ETF which stand at 86 and 88, respectively.

From my perspective, the risk of an investment in MGNR does not just come from its rapid change in net asset value. It is also based on the elevated valuation for many of the stocks that are responsible for its outsized gains. The top holding, HBM, has a GAAP PE of 21.5. Its 10-year P/E average is just 5.05. KGC currently has a PE of 26.0. Its 10-year average is negative. EQX has a GAAP PE of 142.0 while also having a negative 10-year average.

These valuations can only be justified if gold and silver are going to continue to appreciate at historic rates. The question then becomes just how fast MGNR's active management can pick up on the next trend. With a history that dates back less than two years, there is no record to tell us how well the fund can adapt.

Fund Comparison

Last month, I wrote an analysis of the VanEck Natural Resources ETF (HAP). I called the fund a hold, adding that the fund may be worth adding to a portfolio that is seeking increased diversification and inflation protection. I also noted that the fund gives some added exposure to international markets, as HAP has over half of its holdings in international stocks.

Seeking Alpha currently lists no peers for MGNR, but the fund is ranked 14th out of 54 sector ETFs that focus on natural resources. HAP provides a decent comparison fund to MGNR because they have similar objectives, although the VanEck ETF has a greater focus on inflation protection in its stated objectives. HAP is passively managed, following the performance of the Market Vector Global Natural Resources Index. MGNR is actively managed, and investors do pay up for that active management. HAP has an expense ratio of 0.42%, 33 basis points lower than MGNR.

When looking at the valuation of the holdings in the two funds, there is a clear difference in PE ratios. MGNR has an average earnings multiple of 37 compared to just 22 for HAP. The VanEck ETF also has a higher yield, providing a small bit of added cushion should the portfolios from both funds head south later this year.

The reason for the earnings valuation gap is the added exposure that HAP offers to energy minerals (i.e., oil and gas producers). Looking at its 10 largest holdings as of January 22, there is no pure play mining company in HAP's top seven. The eighth stock on the list, BHP Group (BHP), is a diversified miner that is involved mostly in iron ore, copper, and coal. The largest allocation to a gold mining stock is found at 10th on the list, Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM). Of course, like its peers mentioned earlier, AEM is up over 130% in the last 12 months. A more detailed breakdown of the holdings in both MGNR and HAP is shown below.

While MGNR has outperformed HAP over the last year, the performance gap really only took place in the second half of last year when gold and silver related stocks started to really take off. As seen in the graph below, HAP held an edge until about September. Since September 1, GLD has gained 40.67%. SLV has risen by a mind-boggling 150.09%, providing strong tailwinds for MGNR's mining holdings.

I believe this graph gives us our best insight into just what it takes for MGNR to stand out. A historic move in precious metals was able to give the fund an edge. HAP's passive management was able to hold the advantage for the first half of the year, especially early in the year when the overall market was more unstable. Can American Beacon keep this up and justify its higher expense ratio of 0.75% over an extended period? There just is not enough current evidence to support that thesis.

Risks to Consider

There is no doubt that what has powered the outsized returns for MGNR over the last 12 to 18 months is the same thing that presents the fund’s greatest risk. The value of precious metals and other commodities has soared. A report from last October from the World Bank stated that the prices of global commodities were expected to fall in 2026, perhaps reaching their lowest levels in the last six years. Much of this prediction was based on prices for energy and food, but gold and silver, two metals that have supported a significant part of MGNR’s recent gains, are only predicted to post single-digit gains in 2026. For the World Bank’s prediction to come true, both metals would have to decrease in value over the next 11 months, with silver taking a significant cut in price.

The fund’s large share of Canadian holdings gives it some added risk for both currency valuation and geopolitical conflict, as the relationship between the US and our neighbor to the north has become more contentious over the last year. The Canadian dollar has strengthened by about 5% over the last 12 months. There is no guarantee that trend will continue as we move further into 2026.

MGNR trades at a premium to its net asset value on a regular basis. On several different occasions last year, the gap was 50 basis points or more. As I write this analysis today, American Beacon reports the premium to be just over 21 basis points. When a fund is performing as MGNR has over the last year, this anomaly can easily be overlooked, but the fund is more likely to trend back toward par when precious metal prices lose their luster.

The Bottom Line

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating currently considers the American Beacon GLG Natural Resources ETF to be a Buy. With a quant score of 4.43, the fund is almost in the Strong Buy category. However, the biggest support for the stock comes from its recent performance, which has put its momentum grades at the highest levels. No ETF can keep up with MGNR’s pace forever. When the momentum advantages are taken away, investors will become much more concerned with more “boring” topics like expenses and volatility, two areas where the fund does not measure up quite as well.

Neither MGNR nor HAP is designed to be part of a core investment strategy. There is no denying that MGNR has posted strong returns since its inception, but it seemingly entered its sector at just the right time. When investors stop chasing precious metals’ parabolic returns, the diversification of HAP will be even more valuable. It is possible that MGNR will be able to adapt, but there is no track record to support that idea at this time.

I am giving the American Beacon GLG Natural Resources ETF a rating of Hold and suggest investors look at the opportunities presented by the VanEck Natural Resources ETF before making a purchase decision in this sector.

data.title: FB Bancorp Remains Disappointing
data.description:
Most financial institutions that I analyze I end up coming away either neutral on or bullish on. But every so often, one of them will deserve a bearish assessment. A great example of this can be seen by looking at FB Bancorp (FBLA), an enterprise with a market capitalization of only $234.3 million as of this writing. This small market cap is not unjustified. If anything, it's a bit generous. Financial performance for the company, while it has improved some, remains disappointing. Yes, shares are cheap relative to book value and tangible book value. But profitability remains a concern, and asset quality is low.

These are the sentiments that I have expressed about the company in the past. Since my last article about it, which was published in September of last year, the stock has risen by 6.4%. That absolutely matches the 6.4% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. However, since I first rated it a ‘sell’ in May of last year, the stock has appreciated by only 12.4%. That's short of the 17% jump that the broader market experienced. This happens to match, perfectly, my own expectations when I rate a company a ‘sell’. It's not that I expect the stock to drop. Rather, I expect it to just underperform. And underperform FB Bancorp did. Unfortunately, I don't really see a reason to upgrade it at this time. While there have been some small improvements, they are not enough to justify anything other than a bearish outlook.

A mixed bag

In some respects, FB Bancorp is doing a fine job. As an example, we need only look at the deposit situation for the institution. The company ended the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, which is the most recent quarter for which data exists, with $839.9 million of deposits. This was actually up from the $800.7 million reported the previous year. A sliver of this increase came from growth in the wholesale and brokered deposits of the institution from $106 million to $111.4 million. But most of it was organic in nature, driven especially by CDs. This actually makes a lot of sense. As interest rates started falling, some depositors decided to push to lock in rates for an extended window of time. Overwhelmingly, CDs have higher interest rates than most other types of deposits. This also helps to explain why the average cost of deposits for the institution grew from 1.63% for the third quarter of 2024 to 1.88% for the third quarter of 2025.

The growth in deposits that the company saw facilitated a small increase in its loan portfolio. This grew from $750.7 million at the end of 2024 to $753.8 million in the third quarter of 2025. Relatively speaking, FB Bancorp is actually quite diverse. For instance, the firm has a lot of commercial real estate debt on its books. But it also has both fixed and variable residential mortgage loans, construction loans dedicated to the residential space, home equity loans, miscellaneous consumer loans, and more. I always enjoy seeing diversification. But sadly, many banks that are smaller tend to lack that. So that's a positive.

Historically, FB Bancorp has also held a lot of capital tied up in securities. During the quarter, the firm had $298.3 million worth. That represented an increase over the $260.2 million reported the previous year. But it is important to make note of the fact that not every aspect of the balance sheet exhibited growth. In fact, cash and cash equivalents declined from $86.1 million at the end of 2024 to $65.7 million in the most recent quarter. Debt, meanwhile, inched lower from $72.8 million to $71.6 million.

Overall, I would describe the changes to the balance sheet as a slight positive for shareholders. And thanks to this, we have seen some progress when it comes to the income statement. For instance, the third quarter saw the business report net interest income of $13.2 million. That was up from the $11.1 million reported the previous year. A growing balance sheet contributed to this, but also, changes in the composition of the balance sheet, allowed net interest margin decline from 4.21% to 4.65%. This improvement mostly came from the fact that total interest earning assets for the institution remained lofty, while at the same time there was a decline in interest bearing liabilities that management attributed primarily to a reduction in interest expense involving debt.

Non-interest income did drop, pulling back from $5.4 million to $5.1 million. This drop, based on the data provided, was mostly because of a $0.3 million gain on the sale of mortgage servicing rights that the company sold off the same time last year. The fact that this one time event played the decisive role in this change is a positive since this indicates that it was not a broader operational issue. The good news is that this worsening did not prevent overall net profits for the institution from improving from negative $0.9 million to positive $1.1 million. And for the first nine months of 2025, the $2.7 million in profits generated by the company exceeded the $0.9 million loss reported a year earlier.

Because of inconsistency and volatility when it comes to earnings, I really don't think that we can adequately value FB Bancorp using the price to earnings multiple. If we were to just project out results based on what we saw in the most recent quarter, we would get a reading of 53.2, which would be astronomical in and of itself. More appropriate it would be the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. And in the chart above, you can see how the company looks compared to other similar firms when it comes to these metrics. Unfortunately, even though the institution is cheaper than any of these other five banks. Asset quality is enough to offset this appeal.

In the chart above, you can see that the company has a return on assets reading of only 0.09%. That's almost nothing and it falls far short of the 1% maximum that I typically like to see. In fact, only one of the five banks that I compared it to in that chart are worse off than it is. And in the chart below, you can see the same type of analysis using return on equity. The 0.32% reading that we get here is massively lower than the 10% minimum I want to see. And once again, it puts the company near the bottom of the barrel relative to other comparable firms.

There are certain credit quality metrics that are worrisome as well. In the most recent quarter, the company reported that 1.92% of its loans were non-performing. And 1.33% of its total assets were. For context, I prefer readings here of 0.50% or lower. The fact that we get readings that are drastically higher than this, especially when added to the other metrics, is certainly telling. It paints the picture of a bank that's really not doing well.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to be bullish or even neutral about FB Bancorp, I can't bring myself to do that. Shares of the business might be cheap. Or at least they might be relative to book value. Net interest margin has improved and the income statement has gotten better as the balance sheet has grown. But when you factor in asset quality and the fact that revenue and profits have been very inconsistent, I believe that taking a more cautious approach with a ‘sell’ rating is the right choice.

data.title: CBL & Associates Properties: Recent Pullback Gives A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Shares in CBL & Associates Properties (CBL) are marginally down since the start of 2026, underperforming the small gain delivered by the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ). I think this can be seen as profit-taking after a very strong 2025 for CBL, with CBL stock still up 26% Y/Y, notably outperforming the VNQ. Proposals for a 10% cap on credit card interest rates, seen as limiting consumer spending, have also likely contributed to the pullback. The caveat is that further regulation may ultimately be beneficial for consumers as it will leave more money in their pockets (at the expense of bank shareholders), hence the long-term effect on CBL may not be as bad as currently anticipated.

Near-term consumer spending uncertainty aside, I believe the pullback in CBL shares presents a buying opportunity, thus confirming my Buy rating. My positive outlook is underpinned by:

  • A very low 4.6x multiple relative to my 2026 AFFO outlook of >$7.70/share.
  • Significant retained earnings allowing for gradual debt principal paydowns, thus reducing leverage and a key risk in the CBL investment case.
  • A positive macroeconomic backdrop for retail spending, with U.S. GDP seen above the 1.8% long-term average in both 2026 and 2027.

There have been no major portfolio transactions since I last covered CBL. As such, the Company Overview section in my previous article provides an up-to-date snapshot of CBL for readers not familiar with the company. The only change I would highlight is that net debt now accounts for only 67% of the company's $3.3 billion enterprise value, with the proportion of equity financing increasing thanks to share price strength in late 2025. CBL's latest company presentation for November 2025 is available here.

Q4 2025 Earnings Preview

CBL is set to report Q4 2025 earnings on February 13, 2026. I see five key moving parts going into CBL's earnings report:

  • The impact of the government shutdown, which lasted 43 days, likely put downside pressure on consumer spending early in Q4 2025.
  • The tailwind from lower interest payments thanks to 0.75% in Fed rate cuts in late 2025. On an annual basis, these should lower interest payments by roughly $5.7 million, representing a $0.18/share boost to annual AFFO. As the cuts were ongoing throughout Q4 2025, the full quarterly benefit of $0.045/share will only become visible in Q1 2026.
  • The benefit of higher occupancy. CBL's occupancy stood at 90.2% at the end of Q3 2025, up 0.9% Y/Y. This should be enough to boost quarterly AFFO by about $0.05/share
  • Portfolio rotation executed in July 2025, which CBL estimated would boost annual AFFO by 5%. I calculate this should boost Q4 2025 AFFO by around $0.095/share.
  • Debt principal repayments over the past year. While CBL's total net debt was up 5.5% Y/Y in Q3 2025 as a result of recent acquisitions, the company has been paying down debt principal on legacy loans with retained earnings, with AFFO likely to see a circa $0.03/share boost.

The recently extended $25 million buyback program may also provide a tailwind for per share results, although we cannot know for sure what proportion of it was executed in Q4 2025. Conservatively factoring in no benefit from the share buyback, I see the combined effect of lower interest payments, higher occupancy, portfolio rotation, and debt principal repayments at about $0.20/share. As performance will likely show a drag from the government shutdown, the net result for CBL will likely be somewhat below the $2.12/share in AFFO one would arrive at by summing the above $0.20/share tailwinds and the $1.92/share AFFO achieved in Q4 2024.

Hence, my AFFO forecast for Q4 2025 would be around $2.10/share, taking full-year AFFO marginally above $7.00/share.

Updated 2026 Outlook

Turning our attention to the 2026 AFFO outlook, the key tailwind for CBL will be the full-year effect of the 0.75% in Fed rate cuts from 2025, as well as potentially the boost from the first rate cut of 2026, which markets price in for June 2026. I estimate lower funding costs should boost 2026 AFFO by around $0.19/share.

As CBL's major 2025 AFFO-boosting acquisitions occurred in July 2025, the first half of 2026 will also see the benefits to AFFO from portfolio rotation. I estimate these should amount to roughly $0.17/share.

The full execution of the $25 million share buyback (which expires in November 2026) would imply a 2.25% boost to per share results.

The net effect of these three factors would be an AFFO of around $7.55/share in 2026.

CBL's excess free cash flow also allows the company to pay down $95 million in debt principal, which would be enough to boost AFFO by $0.17/share.

As such, even without growth in net operating income or incremental occupancy increases (both of which are rather uncertain), I see CBL in a comfortable position to exceed AFFO of $7.70/share in 2026.

CBL Rating

CBL shares trade at just 4.6x my 2026 AFFO outlook of >$7.70/share. While the low multiple is partially the result of elevated 67% net debt in the company's capital structure (certainly above the circa 40% I see as financially reasonable for most REITs), recent performance shows that CBL can deliver sound AFFO growth even without sizable increases in net operating income [NOI].

As such, even if NOI stagnates in the years ahead (arguably a pessimistic assumption given recent occupancy gains), CBL should see attractive AFFO growth from share repurchases and debt principal paydowns. For example, a continuation of 2025 capital structure dynamics should allow AFFO to grow by about 4.5% per year.

Therefore, I see CBL's low AFFO multiple as a value investment opportunity rather than a value trap signaling a lower AFFO in the years ahead.

The shares also offer a very attractive 33% discount to management estimates of NAV at roughly $53.49/share. As such, notwithstanding risks that I will discuss below, I see the recent pullback in CBL stock as a buying opportunity ahead of Q4 2025 earnings and the company unveiling its 2026 outlook. My rating on CBL remains a buy.

Risks

The principal risk in the investment case is CBL's still elevated leverage, making the company dependent on further rate cuts and favorable financing conditions. Over the medium to long term, retained earnings will allow CBL to deleverage organically, while non-recourse financing also adds optionality on individual properties and associated debts.

A downside surprise in U.S. GDP growth and consumer spending may also result in CBL underperforming my 2026 AFFO outlook. Here the risk appears manageable as the Fed anticipates both 2026 and 2027 GDP growth to exceed the long-term average of 1.8%, reaching 2.3% and 2%, respectively.

Last but not least, a reversal of recent occupancy increases will likely lead to a reduction in net operating income growth. The good thing here is that I have not factored in NOI growth in my forecasting; hence, this risk is arguably mitigated to an extent.

Conclusion

CBL shares are off to a weak start in 2026, underperforming the VNQ ETF. I expect the company to report solid Q4 2025 results, with growth driven by lower funding costs, higher occupancy, debt principal repayments, and July 2025 acquisitions, partially offset by the effect of the U.S. government shutdown.

Attention will quickly turn to the company's 2026 outlook, with my estimates showing that CBL can realistically achieve AFFO marginally above $7.70/share in 2026.

This makes the company's current valuation highly attractive, likely signaling that the pullback is a buying opportunity.

Key risks in the investment case include CBL's high leverage, downside surprises in U.S. GDP growth and consumer spending, as well as a reversal of recent occupancy gains.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Satellogic: Low-Cost Earth Observation Trading At A Discount
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Satellogic (SATL) is an integrated satellite earth observation provider. They design and manufacture their own satellites, called NewSat. They offer satellite imaging services using a constellation of 20 NewSats now orbiting the earth. In addition, they also lease and outright sell NewSat satellites.

To better understand the market opportunity, we need to differentiate between the two types of satellites. First, the Geostationary satellites orbit the Earth from outer space at a speed equal to the Earth's rotation. This speed match allows geostationary satellites to maintain their position relative to the Earth, thus continuously facing one location. The second type is Low Earth Orbit satellites, "LEO", the ones sold by SATL. These satellites orbit closer to the earth, and thus, the satellite cameras can generate much better image resolution compared to geostationary satellites orbiting outer space. But here's the problem. LEOs need to travel around the Earth at high speeds to remain in their orbit, at speeds higher than the Earth's rotation around its axis. This means that LEOs can't continuously monitor one single area.

These operational limitations of LEOs might seem commercially restrictive. Multiple satellites are needed for continuous monitoring, and even more are needed to take continuous monitoring from the same angle. This is expensive for the operator to do and the customer to pay for. However, I believe this creates an environment that encourages collaboration between satellite operators, where unused capacity images of one operator are traded to fill the coverage gaps of another satellite operator who has customers seeking these very same unused spare capacity images.

These dynamics are constructive for a small market player such as SATL.

Market Fit

SATL has 20 satellites in orbit. It notes that most of its images aren't sold (utilized capacity (p.10)). This opens the door for a revenue ramp at low CapEx. The collaborative dynamics discussed above support this premise. Still, for investors, understanding the product-market fit is critical here.

Earth observation has historically been dominated by the defense sector. SALT highlights this fact and notes that 50% of the market is in the US. What's new in the EO market is the emergence of private operators and a renewed focus on cost efficiency.

For investors, higher access to low-cost satellite imaging could spur product innovation that ultimately increases demand for SALT's services. For example, satellite imaging by municipalities for urban planning could become ubiquitous and even streamlined and incorporated as the ordinary course of business. The same thing goes for insurance or agriculture applications.

SATL is trying to capitalize on these trends. Their unique value proposition is based on increasing accessibility by lowering costs. They claim to have a design that relies on off-the-shelf components that are cheaper than peers. Management estimates that if they achieve a weekly remapping run rate, their services will become relevant to more customers, thus increasing the TAM to $40 billion. That might be true. However, as of 2024, the EO market size stood at $3.7 billion, growing at roughly 6% annually. Roughly a third of the market is attributed to the defense sector. SATL's decision to become a US-domiciled entity is partly motivated by its desire to gain exposure to the US defense market. Management estimates that 50% of the market is US-based.

Recent Performance

Before becoming a US-domiciled company, SATL had historically filed its annual report between April and May. However, its last 10-K was filed in March, which is more consistent with the SEC filing requirements timeframes.

In the first nine months of FY25, the company reported $11.5 million in revenue. That's up ~19% from $9.7 million in the same period of the prior year. The growth rates aren't the focus here. Small contract wins can create volatility in growth rates, given the small revenue base. However, the main thing here is the quality of this revenue.

Given the nature of the EO market and the SATL business model, there are two types of contracts: 1) recurring subscription for repeated imaging of a certain location/s. 2) one-time purchase of satellite hardware or images.

In the nine months ended September, the company noted that 21.9% of its sales were attributed to contracts recognized over time, possibly subscriptions. The remaining is attributed to sales recognized at the point of time, such as one-time sales (p.17).

However, this doesn't mean that there is no revenue visibility at all. On its balance sheet, the company records $8.1 million in current contract liabilities, consisting of prepaid revenue for deliveries expected within 12 months. The company also adds in its SEC filings that it has a backlog of $46.8 million (p.18).

The company isn't sitting idle. The backlog and current contract liabilities have expanded YoY. Last week, the company announced an $18 million contract win to deliver two satellites to CEiiA, an engineering service provider in Portugal. Earlier this month, the company also announced a 7-figure contract with a "strategic" customer for monitoring services of a portfolio of sites.

Valuation

Some fellow analysts have criticized SATL for its low profitability. The company burned through $17.4 million in cash in the first nine months of 2025 and reported a net loss of $35.2 million during the same period. My counterargument is this: First, I believe that there is an element of shortsightedness in fixating on profitability for a small-cap company scaling its operations in response to real demand growth, in many cases, prepaid and non-cancellable sales contracts.

Secondly, the profitability margins are heading in the right direction. Net loss narrowed from $45.4 million to $35.3 million in the nine-month period ended September 2024 and 2025, respectively. Similarly, the operating cash burn was nearly halved in the same period, from $32.3 to $17.4 million.

When it comes to valuation, the relatively small market size is a key consideration. The market is valued at roughly $3.7 billion. Roughly a third of that is attributed to defense. SATL doesn't have clearance to participate in the DoD bids. Still, the company does seem to have a critical advantage: low-cost design. It also sells indirectly to the Pentagon via its partnership with Maxar. It also lists Steve Mnuchin, the former Treasury Secretary under the 1st Trump administration, as the Chairman of the Board.

Assuming a 10% market share in the $3.7 billion market, we're looking at $370 million in revenue. Even half of that would still render the current market cap of $617 million relatively cheap, with a P/S value of 3.3x.

Risks

The main risk is that the market might be smaller than estimated. The company's low-cost design might be celebrated in the US and Europe, where low-cost manufacturing know-how is scarce. However, this is not the case in other major space satellite markets, such as India and China. In 2024, India's Chandrayaan lunar missions - the first to land at the moon's south pole - cost a mere $75 million. The idea that the space sector isn't efficient isn't new. Susmita Mohanty, the director of Spaceport Sarabhai, an Indian space think tank, captured this sentiment by stating:

If NASA is designing a lander, their approach is as if, you know, they're designing a Formula One car. But we, we are a more pragmatic country. Susmita Mohanty, Director of Spaceport Sarabhai [3:32]

Final Thoughts

SATL is an emerging satellite Earth Observation service and hardware provider. It benefits from a low-cost satellite design that allows it to compete with market incumbents. The market is growing gradually, but we could see accelerated adoption, particularly from commercial customers, as costs come down. There is no doubt that the Space industry is now enjoying a monetary and technological momentum, pioneered by SpaceX, Blue Origin, and others. Given the opportunity, I believe that SATL is still undervalued at a $617 million market cap. For comparison, Planet Labs (PL), which provides satellite earth imaging services, has a market cap of $8.5 billion at the time of this writing. One can also imagine SATL expanding into the satellite telecommunication market. AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) took the satellite telecommunications market by storm using a similar playbook to SATL - a low-cost, low-orbit satellite equipped with proprietary (and powerful) telecommunications equipment instead of cameras. ASTS has a market cap of $43 billion. The pivot from earth observation to telecommunication isn't straightforward, but SATL has a lot of the infrastructure to do so if it wishes, a prospect that seems completely discounted by the current market cap.

.

data.title: NeoGenomics: Distribution Moat In Community Oncology
data.description:
NeoGenomics (NEO) is executing a pivot from being a commodity diagnostic service provider to being a comprehensive precision oncology platform. While the market has focused intensely on the first mover advantages offered by the MRD monitoring companies, I believe that the fast follower approach taken by NeoGenomics, leveraging relationships with over 40% of active oncologists, offers a superior risk-adjusted return at current multiples. The premise of the thesis is quite straightforward: the most accurate test in the world is meaningless if you can't order it, and NeoGenomics has already removed the friction associated with ordering for the physicians who treat 80% of all cancer patients. Now that the Natera legal hurdles have been cleared and the RaDaR ST launch is about to occur, NeoGenomics will be able to add high-value molecular testing to a large and sticky base of hematology customers.

Recent Financials

The company stated its total revenue for Q3 2025 was $188 million, a 12 percent year-over-year gain. However, the total figure does not reflect the bifurcation of the business model. The core Clinical Services division saw an 18 percent year-over-year increase, or 15 percent if you exclude the inorganic contribution of the Pathline acquisition. This indicates that the fundamental business is not merely stabilized but rather accelerating. This is further substantiated by the company's preliminary expectations for fourth-quarter 2025 revenue of $190 million, which represents an 11 percent increase, and the company's annual top line will come in at approximately $727 million.

Of greater importance than the absolute dollar amount of growth are the changes in the revenue mix. Third-quarter 2025 Next Generation Sequencing (NGS) revenue increased 24 percent year-over-year, outpacing the overall growth of the clinical services segment. NGS currently represents nearly one-third of the company's clinical services revenue. The NGS metric is extremely important because it can serve as a proxy for average unit price expansion and the potential for margin sustainability. NGS tests represent a higher AUP and are generally more sticky than the company's legacy stain or simple PCR test businesses. The fact that NGS is experiencing growth in excess of 25 percent under the company while the broader NGS market is expected to grow in the low-to-mid teens implies that NeoGenomics is gaining share within the industry. I believe this is occurring primarily through the conversion of their existing hematology customer bases to ordering across a broader range of solid tumors.

In addition to evaluating the growth rate of revenue, we need to examine the profitability profile of the company with a level of nuance. The company reported a net loss of $27 million for Q3 2025. At first glance, this appears to be a decline. However, upon closer examination of the company's income statement, it is clear that this was influenced heavily by $7.1 million in impairment charges associated with the planned sale of Trapelo, as well as additional asset adjustments. As these were both non-cash and non-recurring items, they do little to illustrate the operating performance of the business. Adjusted EBITDA remained positive at $12.2 million and marks the ninth consecutive quarter that the company has generated a positive adjusted EBITDA. While the company has not yet been able to generate GAAP net income, the stability of the company's cash position ($164 million) provides the necessary runway to support the commercial launch of RaDaR ST without the risk of immediate dilution. The continued weakness in the non-clinical (Pharma Services) segment continues to negatively impact results; however, as the clinical revenue mix becomes increasingly skewed toward higher-value NGS and MRD, the reliance on fluctuating pharma R&D budgets decreases.

The Community Oncology Moat

Many academic centers have in-house labs and strict, predetermined pathways, while the community-based market is highly fractured, fast-paced, and extremely sensitive to disruptions in workflow. It is precisely here that NeoGenomics gains significant traction. Currently, NeoGenomics holds in excess of 25 percent market share in hematologic diagnostics. Dominance in blood cancers is much more than simply a revenue stream for NeoGenomics. I would also see it as the Trojan horse for their expansion into solid tumors.

As the path of least resistance for a pathologist or oncologist ordering a leukemia panel, NeoGenomics has the opportunity to use the sale of a solid tumor panel or an MRD (minimal residual disease) test as an example of operational efficiencies versus a completely de novo sales presentation. Based on the data provided by NeoGenomics, over 75 percent of community oncologists who were new to NeoGenomics in 2025 purchased five or more tests. This type of land and expand model is very effective. NeoGenomics is reducing the complexity of the vendor list for community practices by providing them with a comprehensive platform that provides everything from basic immunohistochemistry to advanced whole genome sequencing.

Additionally, the introduction of the PanTracer Tissue assay for the purpose of selecting therapy is also intended to target this specific demographic. Community oncologists are driven by guidelines and tend to be cautious and seek high degrees of certainty and speed when making treatment decisions. Therefore, by creating a sales organization to service this demographic, NeoGenomics is establishing a barrier to entry for technology-only competitors. For instance, a competitor may have a theoretically superior molecule detector; however, if they do not have the logistics support, billing arrangements, and EMR integration that NeoGenomics has developed over time, they will find it difficult to supplant NeoGenomics in the community setting.

RaDaR Opportunity: Fast-Follower Strategy

The last two years of narrative surrounding NeoGenomics have been muddled by the legal battle with Natera (NTRA) concerning the RaDaR Minimum Residual Disease assay, but things may finally be clearing up. Natera will drop all appeals after losing its patent claim case in District Court. This is a major event that will clear the way for a full U.S. clinical launch of RaDaR ST in Q1 2026.

It's true that investors tend to punish companies for being late to a market, and it is also true that others have taken an early lead in MRD. But the solid tumor MRD market is predicted to be a $20 billion opportunity, with penetration less than 10%. Therefore, being second or third in a $20 billion market growing at 30% CAGR is not a failure; it is a tremendous opportunity for growth. NeoGenomics has referred to themselves as a "fast follower." A fast follower enters a market that has already been educated by the earlier entrants but uses its larger distribution channel to capture share.

Technologically, RaDaR ST is extremely competitive. With Whole Genome Sequencing, it has a sensitivity of about 10 ppm (parts per million). Ultra-sensitive detection is critical for recurrence monitoring because detecting tiny amounts of circulating tumor DNA can lead to different treatment plans. Published in the ISLB 2025 conference, the proof-of-concept work demonstrates the scientific validity. While RaDaR ST does not need to be 10 times better than what exists today, it simply needs to be as good, or slightly better, and easier to order. Since NeoGenomics already serves the hematology needs of the target population, there is a much smaller barrier to entry for RaDaR compared to the first movers who had to develop entirely new commercial channels from scratch.

Operating Leverage and Margin Expansion

Beyond simply increasing revenue, the company’s long-term plan for improving its margins is well-defined, and I don’t think the market fully appreciates this. The company has outlined multiple ways it will improve its financial results, primarily through gross margin expansion. The company’s lab automation and process improvements are standard efficiency enhancers; however, the consolidation of legacy sites and the company’s LIMS upgrade represent a transformational opportunity for the company to achieve higher throughput while reducing variable labor costs.

Further, the company's transition to higher-value testing will inherently support expanding the company’s gross margin profile. The company is transitioning away from low-margin stain tests to higher-margin next-generation sequencing and proprietary assays such as RaDaR.

The company has also highlighted revenue cycle management and pricing initiatives as additional drivers of improved financial performance. In diagnostics, the challenge of getting paid for services provided can be as difficult as providing those services. The company’s efforts to optimize its RCM and ensure that the company is reimbursed at appropriate rates for its high-value tests represent a critical yet dull catalyst for driving the company from adjusted EBITDA positivity to true GAAP profitability.

Valuation Context

From the way NeoGenomics has been valued, I think there is an obvious disconnect between the company’s rate of growth and how the stock price has changed compared to its peer group. NeoGenomics has a Forward EV/Sales ratio of 2.60x; this is a significant discount to the median for the sector, which is 3.84x, a 32% discount. Additionally, the forward Price to Sales ratio for NeoGenomics is 2.26x, which is a nearly 47% discount to the median for the sector of 4.28x.

The main reason for this discount is due to the fact that the market is penalizing the company for its lack of GAAP net income currently. In addition to GAAP net income, I would take into account non-GAAP financials, including EBITDA and cash flow. Furthermore, if the company does experience double-digit revenue growth (10-11% for 2025) and experiences increased gross margins due to the NGS and MRD mix shifts, then the sales multiple is the most relevant indicator of value.

Additionally, the forward Price to Book ratio for NeoGenomics is 1.94x and is approximately 40% less than the sector median of 3.23x. The market is valuing NeoGenomics near its liquidation value and is providing very limited credit to the company's intangible assets, such as customer relationships and commercial pipeline. With a company that is growing clinical revenue by 18% per year and is clearly headed toward entry into a $20 billion adjacent market, to be able to purchase shares at such a ratio presents a large margin of safety. If NeoGenomics were to prove out the RaDaR launch and re-rate to the median EV/Sales ratio of its sector, then the potential upside would be a lot higher, and this does not even include the compounding effect of future revenue growth.

Counterarguments and Risks

The most prominent operational risk is the decline in revenue within the pharma services division. This segment's weakness has been evident throughout the last three quarters, and although some of the weakness may have resulted from the previously discussed macro trends in pharmaceutical R&D spending, if the trend continues, it could potentially counteract the progress being made by the clinical division and push back the time frame when the entire company reaches profitability. Additionally, the pharma business provides the base level of volume that supports the absorption of the company's fixed overhead costs. As such, a reduction in this volume will have a negative effect on the company's gross margin.

There is also significant execution risk with respect to the company's launch of the RaDaR ST product. While the regulatory pathway for the approval of this product is clearly defined, the competitive environment in the marketplace for the commercial sale of this product is still developing. Other companies, including Natera and Guardant Health (GH), are both incumbent players in this space with large financial resources and established aggressive sales and marketing strategies. In order to successfully compete against these incumbent players, NeoGenomics is relying on its relationships with community oncologists to either supplant them or complement their existing business practices. If the bundle strategy does not work and the physicians who are currently using NeoGenomics for their hematology needs decide to continue to use NeoGenomics for those services but send the results of the solid tumor MRD testing to specialists, then the long-term growth thesis of the company collapses.

Future Outlook

In my opinion, over the next few years, NeoGenomics has sizable potential upside. Most of the legal defense and R&D efforts have been completed for their MRD products, and they are now focused solely on executing the commercial aspects of these products. The preliminary revenue forecast for 2025 for NeoGenomics is an indicator of the potential for this company; the double-digit growth in the clinical segment of their business confirms their strategy to concentrate on the community hospital setting. If NeoGenomics can demonstrate that they can take the high-margin MRD testing technology and apply it to their existing hematology customer base, I expect the valuation gap between NeoGenomics and their much higher-valued peers will begin to narrow.

data.title: Hims & Hers Health: The Reset Nobody Expected
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) is one of the more emotionally traded stocks in consumer health. A 40% drawdown since my last coverage is generally a sign that something is broken. In this case, I see something else. Something that was broken is not the model, but rather a set of expectations that have increasingly moved into fairy-tale land.

I think the question investors are asking themselves is, has HIMS finally topped out as a growth story, or is this the hard part where the platform moves from the growth story to the cash flow story? After reviewing the numbers, the earnings call, and the longer-term guidance provided by the company, I think it’s the latter. That does not make this a low-risk setup, but it does make it an investable one under the right framework.

Strong Numbers, Soft Margins: Why Growth Didn’t Break, Expectations Did

So let’s start with what really happened operationally. Hims & Hers Health’s revenue increased year over year by nearly 50% in Q3 of 2025 to just under $600 million. Subscriber growth was still healthy, ARPU increased, and free cash flow was $79 million for the quarter. These are not numbers of a company with a broken business model. These are numbers of a company that’s gotten large and is feeling some friction as a result.

The tension manifests itself in the margins. Gross margin fell to the 74% level from the high 70s that investors had grown used to seeing. Again, the change was significant rather than subtle, and the stock price reflected that change. However, if I dig deeper into the numbers, the vast majority of the change can be explained rather than being mysterious in nature.

For example, the reduced GLP-1 ship cadence, price reductions to increase access, and the less efficient cost structure related to facility expansions all impacted the business at the same time. Again, management did not attempt to gloss over the fact that these were near-term margin pressures that were the cost of verticalization.

What is more important to me is that operating cash flows continue to grow substantially. The company generated nearly $150 million in operating cash flows in the quarter, exiting with over $1 billion in cash and investments. The balance sheet is not just strong; it allows us to invest aggressively without having to access the public markets, to weather any volatility that may come our way, and even buy back stock when we see dislocations in the market.

I believe that this was the first mistake of the market. It was a mistake to assume that this pressure was not a transitional problem but a long-term one. It was a mistake to assume that this company was not making a long-term choice to reinvest.

GLP-1's: The Risk but Not Entirely the Explanation

Much of the fear surrounding HIMS has to do with GLP-1 exposure. But that fear has only grown more potent in recent times with Amazon’s (AMZN) announcement of its plans to charge high prices on the distribution of brand-name Wegovy. This appears to be a direct attack on what has arguably become one of HIMS’ most rapidly growing segments. Of course, there are risks in play here. Compounded GLP-1 products are in a grey area. Pricing power will not be infinite.

Nevertheless, I think that the market is oversimplifying the dependency. Weight loss, as a growth driver, is a big part of the growth, but it doesn't define the rest of the platform. I think what really jumped out to me from the earnings call, beyond the overall growth, was just how well the company has learned to talk about the business as a lifecycle, not a one-product, one-user kind of thing. Personalization now drives about half of our revenue, retention remains strong, and multi-condition subscribers are now the norm, not the exception.

However, more important is what comes next. Diagnostics, hormones, menopause, and longevity are not incremental additions to the service. In each of these spaces, the service model fundamentally changes. In the case of whole-body lab tests and the integration of those tests into the overall plan of care for the patient, the model fundamentally changes from an episodic model to a proactive model. This model change is important for the business for the following reasons: it expands the market opportunity and reduces dependence on any particular therapeutic area.

I understand the risks of GLP-1, and I’m not dismissing them at all. What I’m saying is that it’s priced as if it’s an existential threat, as opposed to it simply being a part of a larger equation in a platform model.

Growth Is Slowing Because Math Always Wins

Perhaps the biggest misperception about this sell-off has been related to growth deceleration. Revenue growth slowing down from triple digits to less than 50% has been a cause of alarm. But this is a misperception. It is impossible for a firm’s growth rates to remain at early-stage rates once it reaches a revenue of ~$2.4 billion.

In addition, consensus growth expectations for 2026 are approximately 18% growth. While this feels emotionally disappointing, that is only because the market anchored off unsustainable prior comps. From a business standpoint, high teens growth at scale, along with increasing cash flow, is not a failure; rather, this is a transition point at which valuation frameworks should change.

Management has guided towards a $6.5 billion revenue and $1.3 billion adjusted EBITDA goal for 2030. This represents a CAGR of about 30%, which is lofty but not ridiculous, especially if the international business and new specialties continue to accelerate. What investors need is not another 50% growth spurt from HIMS, but efficient growth with a widening moat.

Valuation in Numbers: Expensive on EPS, Fair on Sales

At $29.6, the company appears expensive based on its earnings but reasonable based on its revenue. As the market is expecting $2.35 billion in revenue for 2025 (+59% YoY) and $2.76 billion for 2026 (+18%), the company is trading at 2.9x P/S for 2025 and 2.4x sales for 2026. Paying a mid-2x sales multiple for a company with a still-high-teens-growth business isn’t extreme. The narrative of overvalued is largely based on EPS. 2025E EPS is $0.48 (~62x forward P/E), and 2026E is $0.63 (~47x).

Risks That Actually Matter

There are real risks that should be considered in a regulatory situation in a compound drug, which will have a significant bearing on future revenue growth. The competition from Amazon and traditional pharma distributors will test the pricing discipline, but internationalization risks are execution risks, especially in a difficult landscape like Europe, where the business will be significantly different from the US.

In addition, there is a softer risk that is difficult to model. As HIMS expands into diagnostics and longevity, there is a shift in marketing from discrete, performance-driven marketing to a more brand-based model. This is a disruption in the way customer acquisition costs work. While management seems confident about how they can leverage this in the future, there could be a period of turbulence.

Takeaway

HIMS isn't broken; it just needs to be repriced. While growth rates are slowing, there is real cash flow, a strong balance sheet, and a reset in valuation from sales. Even if margin stability comes through, current shareholders are paying a price that reflects skepticism, not optimism. For patient shareholders, this is a transition phase, and the upside, if HIMS execution remains strong, will be asymmetric.

data.title: TSLW: Big Payouts While Holding NAV
data.description:
The Roundhill TSLA WeeklyPay ETF (TSLW), according to the company's description, is built to provide investors with the potential to generate income and growth.

Roundhill's goal is to pay out distributions on a weekly basis of "1.2 times the total return of Tesla common shares." Other than a distribution of $0.1693 paid out on 1-13-2026, the distribution has been above $0.20 per share, with the majority of them being over $0.31 per share.

For an ETF using leverage as TSLW does, this is an impressive showing on the income side of the equation. Even so, the distributions can significantly vary from week-to-week. When taken on average together, the monthly totals reflect a successful execution of its strategy.

Its share price has been volatile over the last year, ranging from a 52-week low of $25.45 to a 52-week high or $51.90.

In this article we'll look at the strengths and weaknesses of TSLW and what its future looks like for investors.

The underlying

A single-stock ETF like TSLW will perform as well as the underlying, which in this case is Tesla (TSLA). How one views Tesla will determine the expected performance of TSLW going forward.

There's not much to say about Tesla that isn't already known, but the key thing to consider as it relates to TSLW is the overall areas it competes in. For example, AI, energy generation, energy storage, EVs, leasing, and robotics.

With a PE (FWD) of 275, it's obvious that the valuation of the company is based upon future growth expectations, which have been driving up the share price of the company since April 2025, when on April 7, 2025 it hit its 52-week low of $214.25, and has more than doubled to $448.00 per share as I write.

What's interesting about the performance of TSLW is it hasn't participated in the upside movement of TSLA's share price on a consistent basis but has rather moved in a wide range during that time; not holding the top, it has jumped to.

For that reason, even though Roundhill describes the ETF as a growth and income vehicle, I see it primarily as an income-generating holding. Now it wouldn't surprise me if TSLW were to gradually climb in the future along with the upward price growth trajectory of TSLA, but it would probably be at a more modest pace, sustainably lagging behind the underlying.

Also, with the 1.2x leverage, it would seem if it was working well, it would provide a higher floor and ceiling during the time TSLA has been soaring in value.

A look at the chart

While TSLW hasn't been able to consistently and sustainably capture the upside of TSLA's growth over the last year, it has been doing very well on the income side.

For those not familiar with charts associated with high-yielding ETFs like TSLW, the one below is actually a very good one, considering the major purpose of the holding.

The performance reflected in the chart confirms that even with the 1.2x leverage, it hasn't resulted in the overall share price dropping below its floor. And as mentioned earlier, the same goes on the ceiling, where it hasn't been able to break through there as well.

My point is, for an ETF generating the type of income TSLW is, to trade sideways with some volatility, while maintaining its price floor, is a solid performance.

In my research, if an ETF like TSLW is able to trade sideways or slightly above NAV, it's doing the job it was designed to do. The chart below is what most successful high-yield ETFs should look like when they're healthy.

There are some that vastly outperform on share price and income generated, such as the YieldMax Semiconductor Portfolio Option Income ETF (CHPY), which I wrote about recently, but ETFs like that are anomalies, and not the general norm.

What the chart shows is TSLW is more than able to hold NAV while it produces significant income for the investor.

TSLW utilizes swaps with weekly exposure, which is different than its competitors that incorporate daily swaps as part of their income and growth strategy.

Risks

Since the inception of the ETF, we have some understanding as to how the fund performs under optimal conditions, considering the 1.2x leverage it utilizes.

What has yet to be seen is how TSLW will perform when TSLA corrects. We know it is very volatile, jumping in ranges of approximately $8.00 per share to $20.00 per share, and falling back again. Based upon that historical record, it would suggest that when TSLA eventually corrects for a sustainable period of time, it would likely result in the ETF participating in that decline, where the drop in share price could be deeper than when it falls in the TSLA growth period.

But if TSLA continues to grow over the next year or so in share price, the inevitable correction could come from a higher price point, which could mean TSLW may outperform what it has done over the last year, based upon a higher floor.

Where the challenge would probably come would be in regard to its distribution yield, which would drop in conjunction with a price correction of TSLA.

But again, it depends upon how high TSLA rises before that happens, and what the new floor will be with TSLW's share price.

My thought is that TSLW would correct and then trade sideways as it has been during the recent growth trajectory of TSLA. Once TSLA recovers, we'll probably see a similar trading pattern for TSLW as we have seen in the past.

The caveat there is if or when TSLA's promising products start to increase exponentially in demand, which could result in some significant upside for TSLW's growth and income.

Conclusion

I like how TSLW has been performing over the last year or so, especially in relation to its distribution yield. I expect the yield to continue to be volatile, and for the ETF to continue trading sideways to a lot of volatility.

As noted earlier, the chart of TSLW is a good one for what it is designed to do, and those unfamiliar with high-yield ETFs like TSLW should know that this is how charts for these types of securities will look, with the occasional exception to the rule.

If an investor believes in the future of TSLA, then TSLW should be an ETF to take a close look at. I think the growth of TSLA will be extraordinary over the long haul, and those riding that with an income-generating ETF like TSLW, should do very well.

The key is to first, understand what the ETF is and does in order to not be chased out of it because of the volatility, which will continue to be part of the experience of holding it. Those that panic sell can suffer unrealized losses because of not embracing the fact that this is the price that comes with receiving much higher yields than most of the market offers.
